comments.body: Similarly:  how do we add information to an existing bug report?  Several times I've seen an older OpenJDK bug closed with "cannot reproduce" while I'm sitting there with a perfect test case, but no way to contribute anything to the now-closed bug.  It's very discouraging.

It's a pretty crappy situation. Until it's fixed, I have a few workarounds. Post them to discuss@openjdk.java.net (or a better mailing list) or if you are using a linux distro, file it against your distribution's bug tracker. Often we have access to openjdk's bug system.
This has been discussed on the OpenJDK mailing list as well.
bugreport.java.com (based on the link supplied by Karl Richter)

@omajid At this point it will never be fixed, because it's not in Oracle interests to have a good working open source project competing with it's own products and services. That's why OpenJDK need to get their own foundation and git repo and issue tracker. This is getting annoying to a point where it's basically mismanagement and neglectance
The bug submission FAQ list also refers to the OpenJDK mailing lists. I don’t know in which situations one is preferred over the other.
What's annoying is that it's not even possible to follow/subscribe to bugs to see get updates if/when they are fixed...
That seems to be for the Orcale JDK (!= OpenJDK).
OpenJDK is used as base of the Oracle JDK no ? and in their form I could find selection for the package where I wanted to submit
@KarlRichter No. I can tell you from personal experience (having filed hundreds of bugs over the years) that any bugs you report here will get applied against both OpenJDK and OracleJDK.
The page for submitting bugs only has text boxes. Are they expecting me to submit all my classes that are required for the bug to be reproduced into a single text box?!

answers.body:
I wanted also to report a bug and the only place I could find is the following one:

http://bugreport.java.com/

I hope it is the correct place, Oracle engineer might end up creating an issue in the bugs.openjdk.java.net if it happen to be really an bug.

Hope this helps

comments.body: I wanted to submit a bug.. read all that and found myself mumbling "mmm... is this 2005?" feeling a growing urge to jump off a bridge..
.. and three years later here I am again, trying to upvote my own comment.. "why can't I upvode this comment?..oh, right, I wrote it"
I was searching for bugreport.java.com URL, and this answer was too long without providing the actual URL.

We will review your report and have assigned it an internal review ID : 9077273. Depending upon the completeness of the report and our ability to reproduce the problem, either a new bug will be posted, or we will contact you for further information. Meanwhile the bugs.openjdk.org/browse/JDK-9077273 doesn't exist or isn't reachable even for me - the submitter. And no confirmation email was received. Is it 2024 now or 1994?
The page for submitting bugs only has text boxes. Are they expecting me to submit all my classes that are required for the bug to be reproduced into a single text box?!

answers.body:
Go to http://bugs.java.com or jump straight to the report page: http://bugreport.java.com/

My interpretation of their FAQ is that this will be triaged by an Oracle engineer, and if accepted it will be assigned a number and (usually) become visible in both Oracles + the OpenJDK bug databases.

It would be really helpful if this info/link were on the OpenJDK bug page too. The database split is already confusing enough - when your goal is to contribute to OpenJDK but you find yourself at an Oracle-branded page, it seems like you've gone to the wrong place.

UDPATE (Nov 2019): To add Additional information about an issue (FAQ point 08):

File a new incident with the subject line "Additional information to JDK-XXXXXXX'. Replace XXXXXXX with the reference number received when the report is filed. We are working on a system where you will be able to provide additional information to the existing bug.

Now, if you're curious who can get direct access to the bug database...

From (https://bugs.openjdk.java.net/):

Everyone with OpenJDK Author status or above has a [JDK Bug System] account which may be used to create and edit bugs. Those without accounts can view bugs anonymously.

To understand that, you need to navigate the various "role" definitions:

http://openjdk.java.net/bylaws#author

From Section 7:

An Author for a Project is a Contributor who has been granted the right to create changesets intended to be pushed into a specific Project’s code repositories, but does not have the right to push such changesets directly.

From Section 2:

A Contributor is a Participant who has signed the Oracle Contributor Agreement (OCA), or who works for an organization that has signed that agreement or its equivalent and makes contributions within the scope of that work and subject to that agreement. A Contributor may submit changes larger than a simple patch, may propose new Projects, and may take on various roles within Groups and Projects.

and ...

A Participant is an individual who has subscribed to one or more OpenJDK mailing lists. A Participant may post messages to a list, submit simple patches, and make other kinds of small contributions.

So the bar is set pretty high. :-/

I am not a regular contributor to OpenJDK, but I reported 3 OpenJDK issues over 3 years and have a lot of issue tracking experience. It is important to remember that Java is a late convert to open source; most of its existence was as a fully closed product. So, still as of 2025, reporting issues in OpenJDK is indeed unlike anything you might expect from an open source project of that importance. Even if it's not for a major vulnerability, it’s cumbersome.

For mere mortals, I do see at least these 2 ways, and more:

  1. Fill the official public reporting form for Oracle’s Java Bug Database, which―despite its name and documentation―is not specifically for bugs
    • ➕You get an identifier, which should let you see updates later.
    • ➖Your report does not get visible until Oracle triages it. (Taking my latest report as example, this took 9 hours.)
    • ➖You do not get a copy of your report, and cannot immediately read it online (although you should eventually have access to it).
    • ➖You cannot reply to the comments on the ticket.
    • ➖You are technically not the reporter of the ticket, so:
      • ➖You cannot fix your report or improve it.
      • ➖You are not notified of updates.
      • ➖Your report may be quietly altered. For example, you would have to dig in the History tab of JDK-8371312 to notice that Patricia Tavares removed the final paragraph of the description, where I specifically indicated that the report was in the public domain.
    • ➖You are not credited as ticket author.
  2. Write to one of OpenJDK’s mailing lists, such as core-libs-dev or jdk-dev
    • ➕You keep a copy of the report in case something goes wrong.
    • ➖You probably disturb mailing list subscribers who would rather not want to read about every issue.
    • ➖You have to figure out which mailing list is best.
    • ➖You make it hard for others to find your report.
    • ➖You don't get a guided form to fill.
  3. Report to the "OpenJDK distribution" you use (Eclipse Temurin, Debian OpenJDK, etc.)
    • ➕You probably get (direct) access to a proper ITS.
    • ➕You need not check that your issue affects vanilla OpenJDK.
    • ➖Your distributor could take even longer than Oracle to forward the report upstream.
    • ➖You add some overhead for the distributor.

So, the best choice may depend on a number of factors, including priority and the chances you will have to modify your report or provide extra information.

Process:

  1. Search the existing issue tracker(s).
    As OpenJDK and Oracle indicate, the "JDK Bug Database" is public. But it's quite broken when you have no account, and it does not offer creating one. It is easy to access a ticket if you know its identifier. For example, if Oracle gave you identifier 8308254, you will be able to see the ticket at https://bugs.openjdk.org/browse/JDK-8308254
    But to search tickets without logging in, you will need to avoid triggering Jira permission exceptions. It is unclear, but it should be possible from https://bugs.openjdk.org/issues/ if you use the "Contains text" field (not the "Search" field to the right).
  2. If the issue is not already reported, make a choice from the above
  3. Report

If you report through #1, be aware that JDK Bug System (JBS, the Jira instance) is distinct from Oracle’s Java Bug Database. For example, compare the same report in the former versus in the latter, where it is outdated. The intricacies Dalibor Topic explains in this email can be observed by going to the History tab of the Activity section of that ticket on JBS. Submitting more information is not quite as bad as the FAQ suggests. After Oracle processed your report, you should receive an email with a link "To provide more information about this issue". In the case of JDK-8371312, I received that mail after 14½ hours.

Confusion

In case you find contradictions and outdated information in the issue tracking documentation, it can be reported on the discuss mailing list, which is what the JBS Overview wiki page refers to. (And no, mere mortals cannot fix/update that page since wiki account creation is manual.)

title: Where to report issues of OpenJDK when you're not an OpenJDK developer?
body: First I thought it's strange that there's no link to a bug tracker on the OpenJDK project page, then I found bugs.openjdk.java.net, but it's only for accredited project members. How do people outside the ivory tower contribute issues? There's the mailing list and some people call sending issue reports (and patches) to a mailing list bug tracking… but the development and usage of bug tracker contradicts.
